era_context: Late 17th century: European powers consolidating colonial dominance; Ottoman Empire in gradual decline; Qing Dynasty flourishing under Kangxi; the Scientific Revolution advancing in Europe. Religious and dynastic conflicts continue to shape European politics. The Cretan War between Venice and the Ottoman Empire nears its conclusion.

events.title: Treaty of Lisbon recognizes Portuguese independence
events.description: Spain formally recognized Portuguese independence through the Treaty of Lisbon, signed in February 1668. This ended the Portuguese Restoration War (1640–1668) and confirmed Portugal's separation from Spanish rule after 60 years of union. The treaty was a significant diplomatic victory for Portugal and established it as an independent European power. The recognition came after decades of military conflict and was facilitated by Spain's preoccupation with other European conflicts and internal instability.
key_figures: King John IV of Portugal (deceased 1656, but his reign initiated independence)
King Afonso VI of Portugal (reigning at treaty)
King Philip IV of Spain (deceased 1665, but his reign saw the war)

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles by Portuguese and Spanish historians

events.certainty_note: The treaty is a primary diplomatic document with a precise date. The significance and consequences are well-established in historiography.

events.title: Siege of Candia continues in Crete
events.description: The siege of Candia (Heraklion), which began in 1645, continued into 1668 as one of the longest and most destructive sieges in early modern history. By 1668, the Venetian garrison was severely weakened, supplies were dwindling, and Ottoman forces had tightened their grip. The city would fall in September 1669, but 1668 represented the final year of intense combat and negotiation. The siege had already killed tens of thousands and devastated the region.

sources.name: Venetian state archives and military records

events.certainty_note: The ongoing siege is well-documented in both Venetian and Ottoman sources. The intensity and casualties of 1668 are recorded in contemporary accounts.

events.title: Restoration of Charles II in England
events.description: King Charles II had been restored to the English throne in 1660, and by 1668 his reign was consolidating. In 1668, the Cabal Ministry was at its height, with significant influence over policy. This year marks a period of relative stability after the English Civil War and Interregnum, though religious tensions and questions of succession remained. The Restoration settlement was becoming institutionalized, and England was beginning to emerge as a major naval and colonial power.
key_figures: The Cabal Ministry (Arlington, Buckingham, Lauderdale, Ashley, Clifford)

sources.name: English state papers and parliamentary records
Diaries and correspondence of contemporary figures (e.g., Samuel Pepys, John Evelyn)

events.certainty_note: Charles II's reign and the Cabal Ministry are extensively documented in English archives and contemporary diaries. The political context is well-established.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's early reign in Qing China
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor, now in his early teens, was still under the regency of Oboi in 1668. This year falls within the period of Oboi's dominance over Qing politics. Oboi would die in 1669, allowing Kangxi to assume personal rule. In 1668, the Qing Dynasty was consolidating its control over China following the conquest of the Ming. The empire was stable and prosperous, though the Three Feudatories in the south remained a potential threat.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and the Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Kangxi's early reign is well-documented, but specific events of 1668 are not distinctly marked. The regency of Oboi is confirmed.

events.title: Aurangzeb's campaigns in the Deccan
events.description: The Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb continued his military campaigns in the Deccan region in 1668, consolidating Mughal control and expanding territory. These campaigns were part of Aurangzeb's broader strategy to extend Mughal dominion southward and suppress regional powers. The campaigns were generally successful, though they required sustained military effort and resources. Aurangzeb's aggressive expansion in the Deccan would continue throughout his reign.

sources.name: Mughal court records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: Aurangzeb's campaigns in the Deccan are well-documented in general, but specific events of 1668 are not distinctly marked in most sources. The broader context is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Lisbon is sometimes presented as a complete and immediate resolution of Spanish-Portuguese tensions, but in reality, Spain's recognition was reluctant and came only after military exhaustion. Some sources overstate the immediate impact of the treaty on Portuguese international standing. The characterization of Charles II's reign as one of 'stability' obscures significant religious and political tensions that would erupt later. The Kangxi Emperor's early reign is sometimes romanticized; his consolidation of power was not inevitable and faced real challenges from regency factions.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1668 is rich in European sources but thinner for Asia. Portuguese and Spanish sources reflect their respective national perspectives on the treaty. English sources (particularly diaries) provide detailed contemporary accounts of court life and politics. Qing records are extensive but written from the imperial perspective. Mughal sources are less detailed for specific years and tend to focus on major campaigns and administrative changes. Indigenous perspectives on European colonial activities are largely absent from contemporary records.

graph_edges.note: Treaty concludes the Portuguese Restoration War that began in 1640
Siege of Candia concludes with Ottoman victory in 1669
Continuation of Charles II's reign, which began in 1660
